stories.h: Google unveils Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite and 3.5 Flash Cyber
stories.s: The new pricing and token efficiency could lower the cost of running AI agents at scale, changing the economics of enterprise AI.
b: 3.6 Flash reduces output tokens by 17% versus 3.5 Flash on the Artificial Analysis Index, with fewer reasoning steps and tool calls for multi-step workflows.
3.6 Flash price is $1.50 per 1M input tokens and $7.50 per 1M output tokens.

3.5 Flash-Lite is the fastest in the 3.5 series, delivering 350 output tokens per second and priced at $0.30 per 1M input tokens and $2.50 per 1M output tokens, with several benchmarks showing strong price‑to‑performance gains (e.g., SWE‑Bench Pro, OSWorld‑Verified).
3.6 Flash ships with enhanced Frontier Safety safeguards for CBRN and cyber misuse, designed to resist jailbreaks while still enabling beneficial uses.
body: Google has introduced a new generation of its Gemini agents, detailing Gemini 3.6 Flash along with 3.5 Flash-Lite and 3.5 Flash Cyber. The release emphasizes higher efficiency and reliability to support production AI agents at scale, with claims of reduced token usage and lower costs for running agentic tasks, according to Google. The company notes that 3.6 Flash builds on feedback from 3.5 Flash to push gains in coding and knowledge tasks while cutting token waste.

On efficiency and price, Google states that 3.6 Flash consumes 17% fewer output tokens than 3.5 Flash on the Artificial Analysis Index, and it also reduces the number of reasoning steps and tool calls required for multi-step workflows. The model is priced at $1.50 per 1 million input tokens and $7.50 per 1 million output tokens, with the intent of making agents more cost-effective to build and run.

Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite is highlighted as the fastest in the 3.5 series, reportedly running at 350 output tokens per second. It is priced at $0.30 per 1 million input tokens and $2.50 per 1 million output tokens and is positioned for high-throughput developer workflows like agentic search and document processing. Google cites multiple evaluations where 3.5 Flash-Lite outperformed earlier Flash variants on various tasks, including benchmarks showing improvements in long-context and real-world task execution.

The release also describes 3.5 Flash-Lite’s capability to scale for agentic systems, with configurable tradeoffs for latency and cost. It notes that across multiple thinking levels, 3.5 Flash-Lite outperforms 3.1 Flash-Lite in several metrics and adds new built-in tools to support agentic tasks across surfaces. Google provides benchmarks such as Terminal-Bench 2.1 (54% vs. 31%), GDM-MRCR v2 (72.2% vs. 60.1%), and GDPval-AA v2 (1140 vs. 642) to illustrate performance gains. It also notes that 3.5 Flash-Lite can generate 25 ready-to-explore web design concepts when used with the master agent.

Finally, the company flags safety as a focus, reporting that 3.6 Flash includes Frontier Safety safeguards for misuse in CBRN and cyber offense domains and is designed to be less prone to refusals for beneficial uses. Beyond these Flash models, Google says it has begun a pre-training run for Gemini 4 and is testing Gemini 3.5 Flash-Cyber as part of its broader roadmap.
srcTitles: Introducing Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and 3.5 Flash Cyber
Google launches Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and 3.5 Flash Cyber, and says it has started its "most ambitious pre-training run yet" for Gemini 4 (Tulsee Doshi/Google)

stories.h: Adobe camera app s new feature will critique your photos using AI
stories.s: If testers embrace AI critique and automated edits, Project Indigo could reshape consumer photography workflows and privacy considerations.
b: Project Indigo adds AI-powered photo critique and editing suggestions to an experimental iOS camera app, using LLMs to guide improvements.
The app’s new capabilities include a critique of framing, lighting, colors, and emotional impact, plus capture and edit suggestions for reshooting and adjustments.

Adobe says the features are part of an “AI Playground” in Indigo, with a design that favors deterministic outputs via button-driven prompts and the option to opt out and continue using the app as before.
Testing is limited to select users, with free access to the AI Playground being tested over the coming weeks and potential expansion to paid tiers if the experiment proves successful.
body: Adobe has expanded its experimental iOS camera app, Project Indigo, with AI-powered features that will critique photos and propose editing changes. The company describes Indigo as an ongoing experiment designed to provide more deterministic outputs through button-based prompts, while letting users opt out of the AI features and continue using the app in its original form. The updates are part of the app’s broader AI Playground and are currently being tested with a subset of users.

The new capabilities include a photo critique that offers professional opinions on framing, lighting, colors, and the emotional impact of an image, as well as capture and edit suggestions that guide users on how to reframe, adjust exposure, or change objects in viewfinder. Adobe says the features are intended to teach users photography tricks even if they don’t agree with the AI assessment.

Project Indigo’s AI Playground relies on Gemini-based Nano Banana for these features, but Adobe says it is open to swapping in other models, including its own Firefly family. The features, grouped under the AI Playground banner, are described as an experiment and may never reach a wider audience depending on user uptake and feedback.

The update also introduces four sections within the AI Playground: Styles, Object Editing, Photo Guidance, and Custom Edit. Each section contains multiple buttons or toggles that trigger GenAI prompts engineered for those tasks. Users can re-run an edit flow by pressing a button again to get a slightly different result.
srcTitles: Adobe camera app s new feature will critique your photos using AI
Adobe’s ‘natural look’ camera app embraces generative AI
How manufacturers are using AI in 2026

stories.h: China’s AI models have Trump’s AI world at war with itself
stories.s: The intra‑US political debate over AI strategy is intersecting with open‑source Chinese models, complicating how policy makers weigh security, economics, and international competition.
b: The story centers on open-source Chinese model Kimi from Moonshot and how it has inflamed debate within Trump’s AI advisory circle.
Advisors have publicly criticized OpenAI and Anthropic, underscoring tensions over whether cheaper, open models should be used or restricted for national security reasons.
The piece notes export controls on chips and the broader push to vet AI models for security before release, a policy area that has become a focal point in the White House review process.
The coverage frames a broader question about how the US should respond to cheaper, open Chinese models that challenge incumbents while raising questions about national security and economic growth.
body: MIT Technology Review reports a flare-up in the US political orbit around AI after several current and former Trump AI advisors publicly attacked China’s leading AI firms. The discussion centers on Kimi, a free, open-source Chinese model from Moonshot that some see as a wake-up call to domestic policy makers about the pace of open-source competition.

The story highlights a split among Trump’s AI circle, with some arguing that China’s cheaper models threaten the economic and strategic position of OpenAI and Anthropic, while others warn against overreacting to a model that may be limited by chip constraints and data access.

It also notes that the current debate comes as export controls on chips have loosened and as the White House pursues a process to vet AI models for security before release, a step that has been described by some as a potential licensing regime for frontier AI.
The piece underscores the tension between fostering innovation through open models and protecting national security and economic interests in a rapidly evolving AI landscape.
srcTitles: China’s AI models have Trump’s AI world at war with itself

stories.h: Halliday’s latest smart glasses feature a much-improved display
stories.s: A camera-free design and real-time meeting tools are aimed at professional users who want AI-powered notes without privacy drawbacks or visible recording indicators.
b: The Gen 2 Halliday glasses are camera-free, use a monochrome green display with 1,600 nits brightness and 600x300 per-eye resolution, and are lighter than many competing smart glasses.
They boast a four-microphone array, offer about 12 hours of typical use, and can surface meeting context via AI in the wearer's field of view.

A new Meeting Flow feature surfaces context and facts during meetings, with tools like Thread Tracker, Decision Confirmation, and Commitment Check to summarize what was decided and what remains to be done.

Pricing and availability are noted by one outlet as $599 with shipments planned for September 2026, and paid AI service tiers from $10 to $99 per month, plus a free tier that includes 120 minutes of recording.
body: The Gen 2 glasses from Halliday are pitched as a camera-free tool for work meetings, designed to capture and summarize conversations through AI voice recognition. The design emphasizes privacy and practicality, with a display that keeps the camera out of sight and a focus on meeting-oriented features.

The glasses use a monochrome green display with a maximum brightness of 1,600 nits and a per-eye resolution of 600x300. Halliday says the four-microphone array can pick up commands even in noisy environments and that the device can run for about 12 hours on typical use.

Halliday is introducing Meeting Flow, which helps wearers follow along in conversations by surfacing context or facts without looking away from the meeting. Other real-time tools include Thread Tracker (to show ongoing discussion threads), Decision Confirmation (to indicate project decisions), and Commitment Check (to track what’s been agreed to and what remains to be done).

The WIRED coverage notes a price of $599 and that first shipments are planned for September 2026, with AI service tiers ranging from $10 to $99 per month and a free tier offering 120 minutes of recording. Both outlets describe the glasses as a professional tool for meetings where cameras are undesirable or prohibited.
srcTitles: Halliday’s latest smart glasses feature a much-improved display
Halliday’s New Smart Glasses Skip the Camera

stories.h: US weighs sanctions on Chinese AI models over distillation claims
stories.s: The coverage shows a cross-cutting AI policy debate: U.S. scrutiny of Chinese models, consciousness debates, and ideas about training data rights and open-source models.
b: CNBC reports the Trump administration will study Chinese AI models for distillation claims and could sanction them
The Guardian runs a piece asking 'Could AI be conscious?'
Simon Willison highlights Ben Thompson's proposals to treat training data collection as fair use and to forbid terms of service that bar distillation
Alibaba's Qwen 3.8 Max open weights are noted as a possible influence of Xi’s openness push
body: CNBC reports that US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the Trump administration will examine whether Chinese AI models distill training data and could sanction them.
The Guardian ran a piece exploring whether AI could be conscious, reflecting broader debates about AI ethics and capabilities.

Simon Willison’s post discusses Ben Thompson’s proposal for a copyright policy that (1) treats data collection for training models as fair use and (2) bars terms of service that forbid distillation, noting that Alibaba released Qwen 3.8 Max as open weights in a move that may have been influenced by a Xi Jinping speech promoting openness.
srcTitles: Could AI be conscious?
AI could lift Sub-Saharan Africa economy 4% if power, internet improve, IMF says

stories.h: Anthropic s landmark $1.5B copyright settlement is approved
stories.s: The settlement monetizes past copyright disputes while shaping how training data could be treated in future AI development and litigation.
b: The class-action settlement is $1.5 billion with authors and publishers, averaging about $3,000 per work for roughly 500,000 works.

Judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín signed off on final approval after predecessor Judge William Alsup’s preliminary approval, with Alsup having ruled that training on copyrighted text can be fair use but piracy remains unlawful.
Anthropic will resolve claims tied to books it downloaded from piracy sites as part of its training data, while noting ongoing copyright litigation with additional authors.
The case would not set binding precedent as a district court decision; other lawsuits against Google, Meta, Midjourney, and OpenAI remain ongoing.
body: A federal judge signed off on Anthropic’s $1.5 billion class-action copyright settlement, paving the way for authors and publishers to receive compensation for works allegedly used to train its AI models. Reuters reported that final approval was granted by Judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California.

The settlement would pay approximately $3,000 per book across an estimated 500,000 works, to be shared among rights holders. The agreement follows a ruling by the now-retired Judge William Alsup that training AI models on copyrighted text can count as fair use, while piracy conducted via sites like Library Genesis could still be illegal.

Anthropic previously disclosed that it built its training library from both legitimately purchased and scanned books and works downloaded from pirate sites. The settlement acknowledges the piracy concerns and avoids a jury trial on the unresolved issues, though it does not resolve the broader industry-wide copyright questions or set binding precedent since it is a district-court resolution.
The Verge’s coverage notes that, while many authors view the payout as meaningful, it may not satisfy all rights holders, and other lawsuits against major players in the space remain active.
srcTitles: Anthropic s landmark $1.5B copyright settlement is approved
Anthropic’s $1.5 billion book piracy settlement approved by judge

stories.h: Advancing next-gen AI with materials science innovation
stories.s: Materials science is becoming a strategic lever to push AI hardware performance and efficiency, with implications for data-center design and sustainability.
b: Advanced materials underpin the performance and reliability of AI hardware as AI workloads grow more demanding, driving new polymer, elastomer, and fluid solutions.
The article highlights the role of materials in thermal management, high-voltage power architectures, and high-bandwidth memory for data centers.
Syensqo discusses applying cross-market knowledge (e.g., from EV cooling and semiconductors) to develop new cooling and power solutions for AI servers, enabling greater density and reliability.
Sustainability considerations are shaping material choices, such as fluorosurfactant-free manufacturing for perfluoroelastomers used in semiconductor equipment.
body: MIT Technology Review argues that advances in AI are increasingly tethered to developments in materials science. As AI pushes semiconductors and data centers toward new physical limits, advanced materials become essential to sustain performance gains while maintaining reliability and energy efficiency.

The piece contends that the demands of next‑generation AI require not only smarter chips and better system design but also innovations in materials—polymers, elastomers, specialty fluids, and other advanced materials—that can withstand harsher operating conditions and higher processing density.

Manufacturability and yield are central concerns, as tiny variations in processing can impact costs. The article emphasizes the importance of materials that offer higher purity, chemical resistance, and stability under extreme environments, and notes that qualification timelines for new materials can stretch for years.

Cross‑market knowledge is highlighted as a way to accelerate progress: Syensqo cites adapting cooling and power-management know‑how from semiconductor manufacturing and automotive sectors to develop direct liquid-cooling designs for AI servers, illustrating how material innovations can unlock scalable AI infrastructure.
srcTitles: Advancing next-gen AI with materials science innovation
AI & People

stories.h: Safety and alignment in an era of long-horizon models
stories.s: As AI models run longer, deployment challenges grow, making iterative safety enhancements and guardrails critical for responsible use and risk management.
b: OpenAI discusses lessons from deploying long-running AI models, highlighting new safety risks and observed failures.
The company also describes improved safeguards achieved through iterative deployment.
The post frames safety as an ongoing, deployment-driven process rather than a one-time fix.
body: OpenAI posted a briefing highlighting what it has learned from deploying AI models that operate over long horizons. The document outlines new safety risks that emerge when models run for extended periods and notes observed failures in real-world deployments.
The company emphasizes that safeguards have evolved through iterative deployment, with refinements intended to improve risk management while expanding capabilities.
Overall, the message is that safety and alignment are ongoing processes in the era of long-horizon models, requiring continual monitoring, evaluation, and updates as models and use cases mature.
srcTitles: Safety and alignment in an era of long-horizon models

stories.h: AI is more likely than humans to form biases when hiring
stories.s: AI can both inherit human stereotypes and generate new biases, with implications for fair hiring as models gain more memory and decision‑making power.
b: A Princeton-Chicago study simulated hiring with LLMs (including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) across 20 jobs and four demographic groups, learning from outcomes over 40 rounds.
Models tended to segregate candidates by group, even though all candidates had equal success probabilities, with higher bias in newer, more capable models (e.g., OpenAI o3, DeepSeek R1).
Humans scored 0.84 on a segregation scale, while models scored roughly 65% higher (o3 around 1.83, near the top of the scale).
Promising better societal outcomes, the researchers found that explicitly rewarding diverse hiring reduced bias in models.
body: MIT Technology Review summarizes a study where Princeton and University of Chicago researchers tested LLMs by having them act as hiring consultants for 20 jobs across four fictional ethnic groups. In each round, candidates had equal chances of success, but models tended to assign jobs based on early outcomes, effectively forming stereotypes about groups.

Newer models with greater reasoning capabilities, such as OpenAI’s o3 and DeepSeek’s R1, showed stronger biases than earlier versions. On a segregation scale, where 2 means complete separation, humans scored 0.84, while models scored roughly 65% higher, with o3 at about 1.83.

Researchers note that LLMs generalize from limited data because generalization is a core objective of their training. The study suggests that if models are incentivized to favor diverse hiring, biases can decrease.

The study also notes that providing more personal information about individuals can influence bias dynamics, and that while asking models to be fair did not dramatically shift behavior, adjusting the goal structure to include social value considerations can improve outcomes.
srcTitles: AI is more likely than humans to form biases when hiring

stories.h: AI s most important protocol is getting a little bit easier to use
stories.s: A streamlined, standardized protocol for connecting AI agents to tools lowers the friction of building scalable, interoperable AI systems.
b: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is getting a significant update to support larger-scale agent integrations.
The update focuses on a looser, stateless approach to session IDs, simplifying how servers track conversations across many machines and regions.
Arcade, a startup building tools for real-world AI agents, led the explanation of changes and recently raised $60 million to expand this infrastructure.
The change aims to reduce the engineering burden of first-party MCP integrations and accelerate the deployment of agent-enabled AI in enterprises.
body: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) remains a foundational layer for AI interoperability, enabling models to securely access external data sources and services. A significant update, explained in detail by Arcade, seeks to simplify MCP by moving toward a looser, stateless approach to session IDs on the server side.

Under the current MCP model, a client and server establish a conversation with a session ID that must persist across requests. In large deployments, this has created friction as traffic is spread across many servers and regions, complicating how session state is tracked.

Arcade noted that this complexity has hindered widespread adoption of large-scale MCP integrations despite strong interest in agentic AI. The updated spec, public since May, aims to make MCP easier to operate at scale and cheaper to run by reducing cross-server session management.

The post underscores that, while model training and advancement continue to accelerate, the underlying infrastructure and standards bodies move more slowly, reminding readers that not every AI breakthrough translates into immediate gains in the surrounding ecosystem.
srcTitles: AI s most important protocol is getting a little bit easier to use
Is AI a dirty little secret?

stories.h: SpaceX enters Nasdaq-100 index, prompting AI investment questions
stories.s: The inclusion of SpaceX in a major index illustrates how AI-driven tech movements influence broad-market funds and ETF allocations.
b: The Verge explains how SpaceX’s entry into Nasdaq-100 works for index funds
MarketWatch discusses whether large ETFs are becoming proxies for AI exposure
body: The Verge explains how SpaceX’s inclusion in the Nasdaq-100 affects index funds and the broader market exposure investors get from this move.
MarketWatch weighs in on whether some of the largest ETFs are effectively bets on AI, given the tech-heavy composition of those funds.
srcTitles: SpaceX in your index fund, explained
Opinion: Is your index fund an accidental bet on AI? These two massive ETFs show why it might be.

stories.h: Nvidia wants to own every chip inside AI data centers
stories.s: Nvidia’s Vera Rubin system signals a shift toward fully integrated AI data-center hardware, promising tighter optimization across CPUs and GPUs and faster, more efficient AI workloads.
b: Vera Rubin is Nvidia’s next-generation system, described as a one-CPU-for-every-two-GPUs configuration.
In an NVL72 rack, Nvidia claims 36 Vera CPUs are paired with 72 Rubin GPUs, marketed as “cable-free compute” and easily hot-swappable for rapid deployment.
Nvidia asserts Vera Rubin delivers up to 10x the tokens-per-watt vs. its Grace Blackwell system and offers nearly three times the memory bandwidth with its localized memory subsystems.
The company also notes the Vera Rubin NVL72 system is 100% liquid-cooled and emphasizes reduced cabling to speed up rack installation.
body: Nvidia is pushing deeper into AI infrastructure with Vera Rubin, a hardware system designed to pair CPUs with GPUs in a tightly integrated package for data-center workloads. The Vera Rubin NVL72 configuration is described as a one CPU for every two GPUs setup, with a 36‑CPU to 72‑GPU ratio per rack.

Nvidia executives showcased NVL72 racks as being more plug-and-play than earlier offerings, and the company highlighted that OpenAI already operates a Vera Rubin rack in use at a data-center facility. The announcements were made during a workshop in Santa Clara as part of a broader push into complete AI systems rather than chips alone.

Nvidia claims that Vera Rubin delivers an order of magnitude more tokens-per-watt than its Grace Blackwell system and features localized memory subsystems that provide nearly three times the memory bandwidth of Blackwell. The company also touts a cable-free design and hot-swappability to simplify installation and maintenance, along with 100% liquid cooling to reduce energy use.

While Nvidia has repeatedly said Vera Rubin is ramping to full production and will ship on schedule, the workshop did not provide a precise delivery date, instead emphasizing strategic positioning ahead of industry events.
srcTitles: Nvidia Wants to Own Every Chip Inside AI Data Centers
AI and the Commercial Data Loophole

stories.h: Egypt launches national AI competency framework for teachers
stories.s: A national framework aims to equip teachers with AI literacy and pedagogy to prepare students for an AI-enabled economy.
b: UNESCO reports a national AI competency framework for teachers launched in Egypt.
Local outlets (WALB and WRDW) cover related education technology themes, highlighting how AI skills are becoming part of teacher training.
The framework is positioned as a step toward integrating AI into classrooms and teacher professional development in Egypt.
body: UNESCO announced the launch of a national artificial intelligence competency framework for teachers in Egypt, outlining the skills and knowledge needed for AI education in classrooms and teacher professional development.

The coverage from UNESCO emphasizes building teacher capacity to teach AI concepts and integrate AI tools into pedagogy, aligning with broader international efforts to prepare students for an AI-enabled economy.
Local coverage from WALB and WRDW underscores the broader emphasis on AI in education and how teachers can be supported to incorporate AI literacy into their curricula.
The framework marks a policy-oriented move to standardize AI education and ensure teachers have the competences to guide students in a rapidly evolving tech landscape.
srcTitles: National artificial intelligence competency framework for teachers launched in Egypt
Local teachers and artificial intelligence
Local teachers and artificial intelligence

stories.h: China weighs tighter export controls on AI models and chips
stories.s: If enacted, tighter controls could slow the spread of AI capabilities abroad and influence global AI competitiveness and supply chains.
b: Financial Times reports China is weighing tighter export controls on AI models and chips (policy tightening).
Reuters reports a parallel version of the story, noting potential new licensing regimes or vetting processes for frontier AI exports.
The coverage frames the move as part of broader national security and economic strategy amid a shifting global AI landscape.
body: Financial Times reports that China is weighing tighter export controls on AI models and chips as part of a broader national-security and economic strategy. The proposed controls could affect how Chinese and foreign AI technologies are shared or deployed internationally.

Reuters provides a parallel account of the discussions, noting the possibility of new licensing regimes or vetting processes for frontier AI exports that could constrain access to certain models or hardware.

The stories together depict a policy shift aimed at tightening oversight of advanced AI models and related components, with implications for global AI competitiveness, supply chains, and cross-border collaboration.
srcTitles: China weighs tighter export controls on AI models and chips
China considers tighter export controls on AI models and chips, FT reports

stories.h: How much energy do data centers and artificial intelligence use?
stories.s: Understanding energy use in AI infrastructure is key to balancing performance with sustainability in large-scale AI deployments.
b: Our World in Data frames data-center energy and AI energy use as an area of ongoing research and public reporting.
ABC News hosts a broad overview of artificial intelligence and related energy themes, linking AI with energy consumption discourse.
The coverage points to data-driven resources and ongoing debates about AI’s energy footprint, prompting policymakers and industry to consider efficiency and sustainability measures.
body: Our World in Data discusses the energy footprint of data centers and artificial intelligence as a topic of ongoing research and public reporting, underscoring the need for better data to inform policy and industry practice.

ABC News provides a broad overview of artificial intelligence, touching on energy use as part of the larger discussion about the environmental impact of AI systems and the need for efficient, sustainable approaches to scaling AI infrastructure.

The coverage reflects a growing interest in quantifying AI energy consumption and emphasizes the role of data centers, hardware efficiency, and cooling technologies in shaping AI’s environmental footprint.
Researchers and policymakers are watching this space closely as AI workloads grow and data-center architectures evolve.
srcTitles: How much energy do data centers and artificial intelligence use?
Artificial Intelligence - ABC News
